section.title: The Jews Triumph and Purim Established
section.sectionRationale: The dreaded day arrives, and the great reversal is complete: the very day the enemies of the Jews hoped to destroy them, the Jews prevail. They strike only those who attack them, refuse the plunder three times over, and rest. Out of the deliverance a feast is born. Mordecai and Esther write to establish Purim, so that every generation will remember how sorrow was turned to gladness and mourning into a holiday. The rescue becomes a permanent memorial.
section.thesis: On the day appointed for their destruction the people of God are delivered instead, and the deliverance is fixed forever in a feast of remembrance, sorrow turned to gladness by a hand no one can see.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the day of reversal and the victory of the Jews; the second day in Susa and the fall of Haman's house; and the founding of Purim as a lasting feast of remembrance.

units.label: The day of reversal
units.note: the appointed day of death becomes deliverance

verses.text: On the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar, the king’s command and edict were to be executed. On this day the enemies of the Jews had hoped to overpower them, but their plan was {{haphak|overturned}} and the Jews overpowered those who hated them.
In each of the provinces of King Xerxes, the Jews assembled in their cities to attack those who sought to harm them. No man could withstand them, because the fear of them had fallen upon all peoples.
And all the officials of the provinces, the satraps, the governors, and the king’s administrators helped the Jews, because the fear of Mordecai had fallen upon them.
For Mordecai exercised great power in the palace, and his fame spread throughout the provinces as he became more and more powerful.
The Jews put all their enemies to the sword, killing and destroying them, and they did as they pleased to those who hated them.

units.label: Susa and the house of Haman
units.note: the ten sons and the second day

verses.text: In the citadel of Susa, the Jews killed and destroyed five hundred men,
including Parshandatha, Dalphon, Aspatha,
Poratha, Adalia, Aridatha,
Parmashta, Arisai, Aridai, and Vaizatha.
They killed these ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews, but they did not lay a hand on the plunder.
On that day the number of those killed in the citadel of Susa was reported to the king,

who said to Queen Esther, “In the citadel of Susa the Jews have killed and destroyed five hundred men, including Haman’s ten sons. What have they done in the rest of the royal provinces? Now what is your petition? It will be given to you. And what further do you request? It will be fulfilled.”
Esther replied, “If it pleases the king, may the Jews in Susa also have tomorrow to carry out today’s edict, and may the bodies of Haman’s ten sons be hanged on the gallows.”
So the king commanded that this be done. An edict was issued in Susa, and they hanged the ten sons of Haman.
On the fourteenth day of the month of Adar, the Jews in Susa came together again and put to death three hundred men there, but they did not lay a hand on the plunder.
The rest of the Jews in the royal provinces also assembled to defend themselves and rid themselves of their enemies. They killed 75,000 who hated them, but they did not lay a hand on the plunder.
This was done on the thirteenth day of the month of Adar, and on the fourteenth day they rested, making it a day of feasting and joy.

units.label: Purim established
units.note: the deliverance fixed forever in a feast

verses.text: The Jews in Susa, however, had assembled on the thirteenth and the fourteenth days of the month. So they rested on the fifteenth day, making it a day of feasting and joy.
This is why the rural Jews, who live in the villages, observe the fourteenth day of the month of Adar as a day of joy and feasting. It is a holiday for sending gifts to one another.
Mordecai recorded these events and sent letters to all the Jews in all the provinces of King Xerxes, both near and far,
to establish among them an annual celebration on the fourteenth and fifteenth days of the month of Adar

as the days on which the Jews gained {{nuach|rest}} from their enemies and the month in which their sorrow turned to joy and their mourning into a holiday. He wrote that these were to be days of feasting and joy, of sending gifts to one another and to the poor.
So the Jews agreed to continue the custom they had started, as Mordecai had written to them.
For Haman son of Hammedatha, the Agagite, the enemy of all the Jews, had plotted against the Jews to destroy them and had cast the {{pur|Pur}} (that is, the lot) to crush and destroy them.

But when it came before the king, he commanded by letter that the wicked scheme which Haman had devised against the Jews should come back upon his own head, and that he and his sons should be hanged on the gallows.
Therefore these days are called Purim, from the word Pur. Because of all the instructions in this letter, and because of all they had seen and experienced,

the Jews bound themselves to establish the custom that they and their descendants and all who join them should not fail to celebrate these two days at the appointed time each and every year, according to their regulation.

These days should be remembered and celebrated by every generation, family, province, and city, so that these days of Purim should not fail to be observed among the Jews, nor should the memory of them fade from their descendants.
So Queen Esther daughter of Abihail, along with Mordecai the Jew, wrote with full authority to confirm this second letter concerning Purim.
And Mordecai sent letters with words of peace and truth to all the Jews in the 127 provinces of the kingdom of Xerxes,

in order to confirm these days of Purim at their appointed time, just as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther had established them and had committed themselves and their descendants to the times of fasting and lamentation.
So Esther’s decree confirmed these regulations about Purim, which were written into the record.

haphak.gloss: To turn, overturn, reverse. This is the theme word of the whole book gathered into a single verb: the very day the enemies hoped to overpower the Jews was turned around, so that the opposite happened. Scripture uses the same word for God overturning cities and reversing fortunes, and here, though God is never named, the reader is meant to see whose hand does the turning.

nuach.gloss: To rest, to have relief from enemies. After the fighting the Jews rest, and it is this rest that becomes the heart of the feast. The word carries the biblical hope of the settled peace God gives His people once their foes are subdued, a rest that points beyond any single victory to the final rest secured for the people of God.

pur.gloss: The lot, a loanword the narrator pauses to explain, from which the feast of Purim takes its name. Haman cast the pur to fix the day of slaughter, treating the calendar as a game of chance; yet the very lot he trusted became the name of his enemies' rejoicing. Proverbs says the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD, and Purim enshrines that truth in its very title.

basic.context: The day everyone dreaded finally comes, and it turns out the opposite of what Haman planned. Instead of the Jews being destroyed, they are able to defend themselves and their enemies fall. Notably, they strike only those who attack them and three times over refuse to take any plunder, even though the decree allowed it. Haman's ten sons are killed, and after the fighting the people rest and celebrate. To make sure this deliverance is never forgotten, Mordecai and Esther write to all the Jews and establish the annual feast of Purim, named after the lot Haman had cast, a yearly memorial of sorrow turned to joy.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter raises hard questions: why so much killing, and how should we read the violence? The clues are that the Jews only defend themselves against attackers and repeatedly refuse the plunder, which sets them apart from mere revenge and greed. The key questions are what the threefold refusal of plunder signals, and why the deliverance had to be fixed in a permanent feast. The lesson is that a hidden God completes the great reversal and calls His people to remember His rescue forever.
basic.originalLanguages: The whole book is summed up in one verb here: haphak, to overturn. The day meant for the Jews' destruction was turned around. After the battle the Jews find nuach, rest, relief from their enemies, and that rest becomes the heart of the feast. And the celebration is named Purim, from pur, the lot Haman cast to fix the day of slaughter, the very lot that became the name of his enemies' rejoicing.
basic.typology: The day of doom turned into a day of deliverance foreshadows the great reversal of the gospel, where the day meant for death became the day of victory. The rest the Jews gained from their enemies points to the deeper rest secured for God's people. And a feast that fixes the memory of rescue forever anticipates the meal by which the church remembers her deliverance.
basic.christ: The reversal here, where the day of death becomes the day of life, is the pattern fulfilled at the cross and empty tomb, when the day the powers meant to destroy the Son became the day of their own defeat. The rest the Jews found points to the rest Christ gives, and the feast of remembrance anticipates the Lord's Supper, by which we remember a greater deliverance until He comes.
basic.god: God is never named, not even here where His people are so plainly rescued, and that reticence is deliberate. The reader is meant to see His hand in the overturning, the fear that falls on the enemies, and the timing of it all. The refusal of plunder, three times noted, shows a people acting under restraint, not greed. The hidden God completes His rescue and writes it into the calendar so it will never be forgotten.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16 says the lot is cast, but its decision is from the LORD. First Samuel 15 recalls Agag the Amalekite, whose account is closed in Haman the Agagite. Psalm 30 turns mourning into dancing. And First Corinthians 11 gives the church a feast of remembrance until Christ comes.
basic.oneStory: The day appointed for the death of God's people becomes the day of their deliverance, and the rescue is fixed forever in a feast of remembrance. It is the gospel reversal in miniature, for the day the powers meant to destroy the Christ became the day of their defeat, and He has given us a feast to remember our rescue until He comes again.

context.literary: Chapter nine is the resolution the whole book has been driving toward, and the narrator marks it with the theme word overturned. The listing of Haman's ten sons by name, set out one under another, reads almost as a formal record of the enemy's utter downfall. The threefold refrain that the Jews did not lay a hand on the plunder is a deliberate structural signal, distinguishing this deliverance from ordinary conquest and echoing the spoil that Saul wrongly took from Agag's Amalekites.
context.historical: Haman had cast the pur, the lot, to select the month and day for the slaughter, a practice known from Persian and Babylonian divination. Purim became, and remains, a joyous Jewish festival marked by feasting, gifts to friends, and care for the poor, exactly as the chapter prescribes. The difference in dates, the fourteenth for the provinces and the fifteenth for Susa, explains a distinction still observed in the celebration.
context.canonical: The refusal of the plunder deliberately reverses Saul's failure in 1 Samuel 15, where Israel spared Agag and seized the spoil; here the Agagite's house falls and the spoil is untouched, closing an ancient account. The overturning of the day of death is the pattern the whole Bible follows to its climax at the cross, and the feast of remembrance stands alongside Passover and the Lord's Supper as a memorial of God's saving acts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the great reversal completed, the day of destruction turned into the day of deliverance, and to record how the rescue was fixed forever in the feast of Purim, so that every generation would remember that their sorrow was turned to gladness by a hand they could not see, and would never let the memory fade.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a real, once-for-all deliverance and the founding of a specific feast; it does not prescribe the violence as a model for the church, nor command Christians to keep Purim. The abiding truths are God's completion of His rescue, the call to remember His saving acts, and the pattern of sorrow turned to joy.

debates.question: How should we understand the scale of the killing, and does the book endorse it?

views.label: Lawful self-defense with marked restraint
views.case: On this reading the Jews act strictly in self-defense on the one day their enemies had been licensed to destroy them, striking only those who attacked and, three times over, refusing the plunder the decree permitted. That threefold refusal is the interpretive key: unlike ordinary conquest, this is not about gain or vengeance but survival, and the restraint distinguishes God's people from their attackers. The large numbers report the totality of the threat removed, not a campaign of aggression.

views.label: A morally complex episode read against the whole canon
views.case: Others acknowledge the numbers are genuinely sobering and note that the book never has God command the bloodshed, presenting it as the harsh reciprocity of the ancient world. Read this way, the narrative is honest description, not endorsement, and the Agagite thread, closing Saul's unfinished business with Amalek in 1 Samuel 15, frames it within a long redemptive-historical conflict that finds its true and final resolution in Christ, who conquers evil by the cross rather than the sword.

debates.bearing: Either way the text is describing a unique deliverance, not prescribing violence for the church; the threefold refusal of plunder signals restraint and the Agagite framing sets the episode within God's larger story, which reaches its resolution not in the sword but in the cross of Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter gathers the whole book into three words. First comes haphak, to overturn: the very day the enemies of the Jews hoped to overpower them was turned around, so that the opposite happened, and the reader who has watched five chapters of scheming knows whose hand does the turning even though His name is never spoken. Then comes nuach, rest: after the fighting the Jews gain relief from their enemies, and it is this rest, not the killing, that becomes the heart of the feast, carrying the old biblical hope of the settled peace God gives His people. And finally comes pur, the lot, which the narrator pauses to translate, for Haman had cast it to fix the day of slaughter as though the calendar were a game of chance, yet the lot he trusted became the very name of his enemies' joy, Purim. An overturned day, a hard-won rest, a lot that names a feast: the vocabulary quietly confesses the God who is never named.

typology.type: The appointed day of death turned into a day of deliverance
typology.fulfillment: The cross and resurrection, where the day the powers meant to destroy the Christ became the day of their defeat, 1 Corinthians 2:8.

typology.type: The rest the Jews gained from their enemies
typology.fulfillment: The rest secured for the people of God through Christ, who subdues every enemy and gives His people peace, Hebrews 4:9.

typology.type: A feast fixing the memory of rescue for every generation
typology.fulfillment: The Lord's Supper, by which the church remembers her deliverance until Christ comes, 1 Corinthians 11:26.

study.christ: The great reversal completed in this chapter, where the day set for the death of God's people becomes the day of their deliverance, is the very shape of the gospel. At the cross the powers of darkness meant to destroy the Son of God, and the day they chose for His death became the day of their own defeat, for none of the rulers of this age understood, or they would not have crucified the Lord of glory, 1 Corinthians 2:8. The rest the Jews gained from their enemies points beyond itself to the rest that remains for the people of God, Hebrews 4:9, won by the One who has subdued every foe. And the feast that fixed the memory of the rescue for every generation anticipates the Supper the Lord gave His church, by which we proclaim His saving death until He comes, 1 Corinthians 11:26. Purim remembers a deliverance from Haman; the gospel feast remembers a deliverance from sin and death itself.
study.god: God is never named, not even in the chapter where His people are so clearly and completely rescued, and this studied silence is the book's boldest move. The reader is left to see His hand everywhere: in the day that was overturned, in the fear that fell on the enemies and the officials alike, in the timing that gave the Jews the victory. The threefold note that they did not lay a hand on the plunder shows a people acting with restraint rather than greed, distinguishing this deliverance from mere conquest. And the hidden God does not merely rescue and move on; He sees the rescue written into the calendar so that His faithfulness will be told to every generation. The God who works unseen wants to be remembered.

crossReferences.note: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD, the truth enshrined in the name Purim.
Saul spared Agag the Amalekite and took the spoil; here the Agagite's house falls and the spoil is refused, closing the old account.
God turns mourning into dancing, the very reversal the feast of Purim commemorates.
The church proclaims the Lord's death until He comes, a feast of remembrance greater than Purim.

study.oneStory: The day appointed for the destruction of God's people becomes the day of their deliverance, and the rescue is fixed forever in a feast so the memory will never fade. It is the gospel reversal in miniature, for the day the powers meant to destroy the Christ became the day of their defeat, and He has given His people a feast of remembrance and a rest that will never end, to be enjoyed until He comes again.

sermon.title: The Tables Are Turned
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Esther, this message expounds Esther 9, the day of reversal in which the Jews are delivered and their enemies fall, tracing the unseen providence of God who turns the tables for His people; the founding of Purim in the same chapter is handled in the companion message of the series. A faithful, expository handling of this movement.

quiz.q: What happened on the thirteenth of Adar, the day set for the Jews' destruction?

options.text: The day was overturned, and the Jews prevailed over those who hated them.
options.note: Esther 9:1 states the enemies' plan was overturned; the reversal is complete.

options.text: The Jews were nearly destroyed but escaped at the last moment.
options.note: They were not nearly destroyed; the day was fully turned in their favor.

options.text: The king cancelled the day entirely and no fighting occurred.
options.note: The day still came, but the outcome was reversed; the Jews defended themselves.

options.text: Haman rose again to lead a final assault.
options.note: Haman was already dead; his plot recoiled and his people fell.

quiz.q: What detail is repeated three times to mark the character of the Jews' victory?

options.text: They did not lay a hand on the plunder.
options.note: Esther 9:10, 15, 16 all stress their refusal of spoil, a mark of restraint.

options.text: They spared every woman and child.
options.note: Restraint is emphasized, but the repeated refrain is specifically about the plunder.

options.text: They fought only at night.
options.note: There is no such detail; the repeated note is the refusal of plunder.

options.text: They asked the king's permission before each battle.
options.note: The threefold refrain concerns the plunder they refused, not repeated permissions.

quiz.q: Why was the feast of Purim established?

options.text: To fix the memory of the deliverance forever, so every generation would remember sorrow turned to joy.
options.note: Esther 9:27-28 makes remembrance the heart of the feast.

options.text: To celebrate Mordecai's rise to power in the palace.
options.note: The feast remembers the whole people's deliverance, not merely Mordecai's honor.

options.text: To mark the death of Haman's ten sons alone.
options.note: Their fall is part of it, but the feast commemorates the rescue of the whole people.

options.text: To replace the Passover with a new festival.
options.note: Purim did not replace Passover; it is a distinct memorial of this deliverance.

quiz.q: Where does the name Purim come from?

options.text: From the pur, the lot Haman cast to fix the day of slaughter.
options.note: Esther 9:24-26 explains the feast is named after the lot Haman cast.

options.text: From the Hebrew word for deliverance.
options.note: It sounds fitting, but the text names it from pur, the lot, not from deliverance.

options.text: From the name of the month of Adar.
options.note: The feast falls in Adar, but its name comes from the lot, the pur.

options.text: From the Persian word for feast.
options.note: The narrator explicitly derives the name from pur, the lot Haman cast.

quiz.q: How does the great reversal of this chapter point to Christ?

options.text: The day meant for death became the day of deliverance, fulfilled when the cross became the defeat of the powers.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 2:8 shows the day of the cross undoing the powers that sought it.

options.text: The killing of the enemies models how the church should treat its opponents.
options.note: The chapter is descriptive; Christ conquers by the cross, not the sword.

options.text: Mordecai's fame is a direct prophecy of Christ's kingship.
options.note: Mordecai's rise matters, but the christological center is the reversal of the day of death.

options.text: The chapter has no connection to the gospel.
options.note: The turning of the day of death into deliverance runs straight to the cross and resurrection.

questions: The day meant for destruction became the day of deliverance. Where in your life do you need to trust that God can completely reverse a situation that looks hopeless?
The Jews refused to take the plunder, again and again, even when it was permitted. Where might God be calling you to restraint, to want only what is right rather than all you are allowed?
Mordecai and Esther made sure the rescue would never be forgotten. What has God done in your life that you are in danger of forgetting, and how could you build in a way to remember it?
Purim turns a day of dread into a day of gifts to friends and care for the poor. How does remembering God's deliverance move you toward generosity rather than mere relief?
The lot Haman trusted became the name of his enemies' joy. Where are you tempted to fear chance and circumstance, when Scripture says even the lot is governed by the Lord?

translationNotes.note: The plan was overturned renders the Hebrew haphak, to turn or reverse, the theme word of the whole book gathered into a single verb at the moment of deliverance.

The refrain they did not lay a hand on the plunder, repeated in verses 15 and 16, is a deliberate structural signal of restraint, echoing and reversing Saul's seizure of Amalekite spoil in 1 Samuel 15.
The narrator pauses to explain the loanword Pur as the lot, from which the feast of Purim is named; the parenthesis is in the original and requires no dash.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Esther 9:1-32. No dashes were present in the verse text; the parenthesis in verse 24 is original. Sermon verified this session from truthforlife.org (The Tables Are Turned, Esther 9:1-19; the Purim material of 9:20-32 is covered in the companion series message).
